skill.name: twitter-automation
skill.description: Automates Twitter/X posting, engagement, direct messages, follows, profile lookups, and media posts through the inference.sh CLI.
skill.summary: Run Twitter/X account actions through inference.sh app commands.
skill.author: inference-sh-8

security_audit.summary: The Ruby backtick and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown fences, inline code, and Twitter/X wording. The pipe-to-shell installer is a true positive because line 17 downloads and executes a remote script. The skill also performs live Twitter/X account actions through an external CLI, so publication should require human review and stronger installation guidance.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
high_findings.description: Line 17 instructs users to download an install script from https://cli.inference.sh and pipe it directly to sh. This executes remote code before the user can inspect it, and the later checksum note does not protect this command path.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact command uses curl piped into sh, which is a direct remote code execution pattern. The install note claims verification is available, but the quick start still executes the script without prior review.

medium_findings.title: Live Social Media Account Actions
medium_findings.description: The skill documents commands that post, like, retweet, delete posts, send direct messages, and follow users through an external CLI. These actions can alter a user's public account or send messages if executed without explicit confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The app list and examples clearly show commands for account-changing Twitter/X actions. The behavior is intentional for this skill, but it creates material misuse and accidental-action risk.

medium_findings.title: External CLI and Network Service Dependency
medium_findings.description: The skill relies on the inference.sh CLI and external inference.sh, dist.inference.sh, and documentation URLs. Users must trust the external service, its authentication flow, and the app implementations behind each command.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly links to inference.sh services and runs the infsh CLI. No credential exfiltration is shown, but the skill depends on external network execution.

medium_findings.title: Related Skill Installation Commands
medium_findings.description: The related skills section includes npx skills add commands for installing additional packages. These commands can expand the trusted code surface beyond the reviewed Twitter automation skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are presented as optional related installs, not hidden execution. They still introduce third-party installation risk that is outside this audit scope.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The external command scanner flagged Markdown code fences and inline command examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. No Ruby source file or Ruby backtick operator is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited syntax is Markdown fencing around examples. The reviewed file is SKILL.md, and I found no Ruby execution context.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to the skill description and a Markdown table header. I found no MD5, SHA-1, insecure cipher use, or cryptographic implementation at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain Twitter/X descriptive text and a table header. The only checksum reference is SHA-256 on line 23, which is not a weak algorithm.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Install Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The command downloads a remote installer and immediately executes it with sh. This pattern should be replaced with a manual download, checksum verification, and explicit execution step.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is a direct curl-to-shell pattern. It is user-visible documentation rather than hidden malware, so it is high risk but not blocked as confirmed malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Account-Changing External Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples run external CLI commands that can create posts, delete posts, send direct messages, like, retweet, and follow accounts. These commands need explicit user approval before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly invoke infsh app run commands for Twitter/X account actions. The risk depends on user intent and account permissions.

content.value_statement: Managing routine Twitter/X actions can take time and create repeated manual work. This skill provides ready CLI workflows for posting, media sharing, engagement, messages, follows, and profile lookups.
actual_capabilities: Posts text updates through the x/post-tweet app.
Creates media posts through the x/post-create app.
Likes, retweets, deletes, and retrieves posts by ID.
Sends direct messages and follows users.
Combines generated image or video outputs with Twitter/X posting.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, login, and a configured Twitter/X integration.
Runs live account actions and does not include a built-in dry run mode.
Does not provide campaign analytics or audience strategy.
Media posts require a reachable media URL.

use_cases.title: Publish Product Updates
use_cases.description: Prepare and post short release announcements or media updates from a command workflow.

use_cases.title: Manage Routine Engagement
use_cases.description: Like, retweet, follow, or retrieve posts and profiles during planned social media operations.

use_cases.title: Test X Integration Flows
use_cases.description: Run controlled post, media, and user lookup examples while validating a Twitter/X integration.

prompt_templates.title: Post a Basic Update
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a short Twitter/X post for this update, then show the exact infsh command I should review before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Media Post
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a Twitter/X media post using this message and media URL. Include a concise caption and the command to post it.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Engagement Actions
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare safe commands to like, retweet, or inspect these post IDs. Separate account-changing actions from read-only actions.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Generated Media Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a workflow that generates media, captures the output URL, and creates a Twitter/X post after my approval.

output_examples.input: Post a short launch note for a new dashboard feature.
output_examples.output: A concise tweet draft, followed by a single reviewed command that posts the text through x/post-tweet.

output_examples.input: Share an image URL with a caption about an AI art experiment.
output_examples.output: A media-ready caption and an x/post-create command that uses the supplied image URL.

output_examples.input: Check a user profile before deciding whether to follow.
output_examples.output: A read-only profile lookup command first, with the follow command listed separately for explicit approval.

best_practices: Review every generated command before running it against a live account.
Use manual CLI installation and checksum verification instead of pipe-to-shell installation.
Separate read-only lookups from actions that post, message, delete, or follow.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated posting or messaging commands without reviewing the final text.
Do not automate likes, retweets, follows, or messages in a way that violates platform rules.
Do not pass secrets, session tokens, or private account data in prompt text.

faq.question: Does this skill post directly to Twitter/X?
faq.answer: It provides infsh commands that can post through a configured Twitter/X integration.

faq.question: Can it post images or videos?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented x/post-create app accepts media URLs for media posts.

faq.question: Does it require authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. Users need the inference.sh CLI, login, and a connected Twitter/X account.

faq.question: Is the installation method risk free?
faq.answer: No. The quick start uses a pipe-to-shell installer, which should be replaced with manual verification.

faq.question: Can it read Twitter/X profiles or posts?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for retrieving users and posts through app commands.

faq.question: Is it safe for unattended automation?
faq.answer: No. Account-changing actions should require explicit review and approval before execution.
